does: This paper introduces Steerable Cultural Preference Optimization (SCPO), a novel reward model training algorithm designed to incorporate and balance diverse cultural preferences in large language models. Rather than optimizing for a single unified preference distribution, SCPO trains reward models that can accurately represent the values and preferences of multiple cultural subcommunities simultaneously. The method is evaluated on two datasets (PRISM and GlobalOpinionQA) across seven countries, demonstrating improvements of up to 7 points for minority-group reward models compared to baseline approaches. The algorithm achieves 280% greater training data efficiency than standard full-data finetuning, making it practical for scenarios where preference data from different communities may be limited or unbalanced.
connects: This work extends recent research in LLM alignment and reward modeling that has grappled with the challenge of representing diverse human preferences. It builds on prior methods for training reward models from human feedback while addressing a critical gap: most existing alignment research treats preference data as a unified signal, often reflecting the values of annotators from privileged regions or majority groups. SCPO responds to growing awareness that single-preference-model approaches can embed cultural bias and fail minority communities. The paper connects to concurrent work on fairness in machine learning and the emerging subfield of culturally-aware NLP, combining techniques from preference learning with methods for balancing data across groups.
enables: This work enables the development of LLM systems that can serve genuinely global audiences without forcing cultural homogenization or marginalizing minority preferences. It points toward reward models as a key mechanism for implementing cultural pluralism in AI systems, suggesting future work on scaling SCPO to more countries, languages, and cultural dimensions. The data efficiency gains open possibilities for collaborative alignment where communities can contribute preference data without requiring massive annotation efforts. This foundation could support new approaches to international AI governance, community-led model customization, and frameworks for evaluating whether deployed systems respect the values of all populations they serve. The method also raises questions about how to identify and balance cultural subcommunities meaningfully.
